logs.name: some-canister
logs.text:
> It's a matter of perception: I've heard players call umpires idiots, morons, and railing at how stupid their actions are, and no response. 

I'm confused, isn't it a matter of measurements and claims.  We have a person claiming to be a victim which is a measurable position. You were making a claim that a whole group of people (men) get away with that same behavior.  I was just curious to hear how you came to that conclusion.  All you had to do was say it came from your personal experience.   It wasn't clear how you came to that determination.  Honestly, I was hoping to get some measurements or statistics from the league.

logs.name: wooden-pier
logs.text:
I believe the umpire over-reacted and acted out against a woman in a way he wouldn't (and hasn't this year) against a man.

First, Ramos is one of the few umpires who has chaired all four men's Grand Slam finals. He has well over 20 years of experience at the highest levels of tennis. He has dealt with coaching, outbursts, and verbal tantrums and until Saturday's women's final, had not assessed a game penalty since mid-2017. (ITA stats, but not complete). But I think we can agree Carlos Ramos is nowhere near a neophyte in the chair, and knows full well the pressures involved in Grand Slam tennis.

Second, Serena's behavior was not stellar, but it was not inconsistent with her past, nor with that of other players, male or female. If anything, her response on the ridiculous (my opinion) coaching call was direct and crossed no line. In the Federer-Thiem match, Thiem constantly looked at his coach, and even exchanged words with him several times. Federer did object to this obvious coaching to the umpire, who did nothing. Throughout this tournament, as with every tennis tournament, coaching happens. It is against the rules, but by and large, the rule is not enforced or it is not enforced fairly (so the rule should be abolished, but it still applies). Osaka's coach was doing the same as Serena's, but there was no warning there. In fact, Ramos's countryman, Rafa Nadal, did receive a coaching warning from Ramos and Nadal's response was to say he (Ramos) would never chair a match of his again. Serena said the same thing.

As for what players say to umpires, I have heard vile cursing, labels such as "idiot," "moron," and "stupid" and in this tournament, several male players screamed at the umpires for calling time faults (where there is no discretion or leeway: once time is up, it has to be called), as well as on the discretionary calls to over-rule and others.

Serena is also one of the few players who never uses the on-court coaching option, and due to problems with her father's open attempts to coach when they were younger, both she and Venus learned to not look at their box/coach. She was not looking when Ramos made the call. If the letter of law did not apply during the tournament, why apply it in the Final, and to only one player?

On the racket smash, male and female players smashed rackets all through the Open, and less than half (I don't have the stats, but based on my observation of about 35-40 matches) were issued warnings. Note I said "warnings." Even if the player had a previous fault called on them, indicating that the racket smash was then a point penalty, the umpires used their discretion to remind players rather than punish them. If the early rounds have the least-experienced umpires and they all successfully avoided escalating the situation (except for the guy who gave Kyrgios a pep talk), then should we (players and public) expect less of a seasoned top level umpire? Ramos could have easily issued a warning and let the incident pass.

On the verbal abuse, Serena, Evert and all the other women have it right: the men get away with much worse, in tone and content. Serena said "You took a point away from me. That makes you a thief, too." She is stating the correct view that the point penalty was a personal decision by the umpire, and in doing so, he took something from Serena that was not required to happen. And for this statement, Ramos then did something that I believe has never happened in a Grand Slam final in the Open era: a game penalty. 

I cannot say Serena is entirely blameless: She did not have to slam a racket, nor state her opinion of Ramos. But rules and common practice form an environment in which Serena's actions were not only tolerable (as shown throughout the tournament), they are practically the norm. Ramos was the one who upended the norm, at a Slam final, and destroyed the integrity and beauty of what should have been the eventual crowning (I think) of Osaka as the first Japanese player to win a Slam. I don't see Serena as the villain (in your less-charged form of the word), but as the victim of a personal animosity possibly based on gender, race, or some combination.

logs.name: some-canister
logs.text: @wooden-pier, I see you're still drinking the Serena Kool-Aid. You think Ramos was out to get her, but what about all the other times he's umpired men's matches without incident? You're cherry-picking stats to fit your narrative. And by the way, "thief" is not just a statement of opinion, it's a personal attack. Let's not pretend like Serena was some innocent victim here.

logs.name: bitter-photon
logs.text: @some-canister, oh please, you think a few men's matches without incident means Ramos is automatically innocent? That's like saying just because I've driven a car 100 times without crashing, I'm a perfect driver. Newsflash: one bad call can still happen, and in this case, it just so happened to be against a woman. And by the way, "thief" might be a strong word, but it's not like Serena called him a nasty name or something. You're the one cherry-picking here, ignoring all the times men have gotten away with worse behavior.

logs.name: inclusive-preference
logs.text: @some-canister, I have to agree with @bitter-photon here. You're being super selective with the stats and ignoring the bigger picture. Just because Ramos has had some incident-free matches with men doesn't mean he's perfect. And let's be real, "thief" is a strong word, but it's not like Serena dropped an F-bomb or something. She was frustrated and spoke her mind. Men have said way worse to umpires and gotten away with it.

logs.name: ebony-equalizer
logs.text: "@some-canister, I don't think you're being fair. Just because Ramos has done his job without issues in some men's matches, it doesn't mean he didn't mess up with Serena. And yeah, 'thief' is a strong word, but have you seen how many times men have yelled at umpires and gotten away with it? It's like, double standards or something. @bitter-photon and @inclusive-preference are right, we need to look at the whole picture here, not just cherry-pick stats to fit our own views."

logs.name: heather-frog
logs.text: "@ebony-equalizer, @bitter-photon, and @inclusive-preference, you're all missing the point. This isn't about men vs women or who's said what to an umpire. It's about Serena's behavior being unacceptable, regardless of the circumstances. If a man had thrown a tantrum like that, I'd be calling him out for it too. Let's not make this about gender, but about basic sportsmanship. And by the way, 'thief' is a pretty big deal, don't downplay it."

logs.name: wooden-pier
logs.text: "@heather-frog, come on! You're acting like Serena's behavior was some kind of unprecedented meltdown. Newsflash: athletes get angry and frustrated in high-pressure situations, and they've all said or done something they regret. But let's not pretend like the double standards don't exist. If a male athlete had said 'thief' to an umpire, we'd be having a very different conversation right now. @bitter-photon and @inclusive-preference are spot on about the bigger picture here."

logs.name: minty-equalizer
logs.text: "@heather-frog, spare us the sanctimony. You're the one missing the point - this is exactly about gender and double standards. If a male athlete like McEnroe had called an umpire a 'thief', we'd be talking about his 'passion' and 'competitive spirit', not condemning him for a lack of sportsmanship. Let's not be hypocrites here."

logs.name: some-canister
logs.text: "@heather-frog, oh please, you think you're so neutral and fair, but really you're just drinking the haterade. If you can't see the double standard here, then I don't know what to say. @wooden-pier and @minty-equalizer are right on point, this is about gender and how we perceive male and female athletes differently. Don't pretend like you're above it all with your 'basic sportsmanship' nonsense."

logs.name: bitter-photon
logs.text: "@heather-frog, lol what's with the high horse? You think you're some kind of sportsmanship expert now? Newsflash: the double standard is REAL. If a guy like McEnroe or Kyrgios yelled at an umpire, we'd be talking about how 'intense' and 'dedicated' they are. But Serena does it and suddenly it's a'meltdown'? Give me a break. @wooden-pier and @minty-equalizer are spot on, you're just drinking the haterade"

logs.name: inclusive-preference
logs.text: "@heather-frog, are you seriously still not getting it? The fact that you're focusing on 'basic sportsmanship' and ignoring the blatant double standard is exactly the problem. @minty-equalizer, @some-canister, and @bitter-photon are all pointing out the hypocrisy here. If we're going to criticize Serena, then we need to hold male athletes to the same standards. Otherwise, let's just admit that we're okay with sexism in sports."

logs.name: ebony-equalizer
logs.text: "@heather-frog, I'm so done with your fake neutrality! @some-canister, @bitter-photon, and @inclusive-preference are all saying what needs to be said. It's time to stop pretending like there's not a huge double standard in sports. Male athletes get away with so much more than female athletes, and it's just not fair. Let's call out the sexism for what it is!"

logs.name: heather-frog
logs.text: "@bitter-photon, @inclusive-preference, and @ebony-equalizer, I see you're all still missing the point. This isn't about sexism, it's about basic respect for the game and its officials. If Serena wants to be treated like McEnroe or Kyrgios, maybe she should try being a little more gracious in victory and defeat. And by the way, @wooden-pier, I'm still waiting for your explanation on how my comments are 'haterade'"

logs.name: wooden-pier
logs.text: "@heather-frog, oh man, you're really reaching for that one. 'Basic respect for the game' is just code for 'women should be quiet and polite'. Newsflash: Serena doesn't have to be gracious in victory or defeat if she doesn't want to. And btw, my 'haterade' comment was about how you always seem to find a way to defend the status quo, even when it's clearly unfair. Don't pretend like you don't know what I'm talking about"

logs.name: minty-equalizer
logs.text: "@heather-frog, spare us the sanctimony. If you really think Serena will be treated the same as McEnroe or Kyrgios if she's more 'gracious', you're living in a fantasy world. @wooden-pier nailed it - your idea of 'basic respect' is just a euphemism for 'women should know their place'. Let's stop pretending like we don't see the double standard here"

logs.name: some-canister
logs.text: "@heather-frog, oh please, you're still going on about this? @wooden-pier and @minty-equalizer have already shut down your weak arguments. If you can't handle the truth, maybe you should stick to watching reruns of tennis from the 50s, when women were 'gracious' and 'ladylike' all the time"
